---
format:
    revealjs:
        theme: ../../styles/meds-slides-styles.scss
        slide-number: true
        chalkboard: true
        title-slide: false
jupyter: eds232-env
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
from itertools import combinations
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
import statsmodels.api as sm

plt.style.use('default')
fig_size_x = 8
fig_size_y = 4
plt.rcParams['font.size'] = 10
plt.rcParams['legend.fontsize'] = 'x-large'

np.random.seed(7)
n = 200

process    = np.random.uniform(0, 300, n)
efficiency = np.random.uniform(0,  50, n)
offsets    = np.random.uniform(0, 100, n)

co2_reduction = (2.939
                 + 0.046  * process
                 + 0.189  * efficiency
                 - 0.001  * offsets
                 + np.random.normal(0, 1.69, n))

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'process':       process,
    'efficiency':    efficiency,
    'offsets':       offsets,
    'co2_reduction': co2_reduction
})

# Individual SLR fits
X_proc = sm.add_constant(df['process'])
res_proc = sm.OLS(df['co2_reduction'], X_proc).fit()

X_eff = sm.add_constant(df['efficiency'])
res_eff = sm.OLS(df['co2_reduction'], X_eff).fit()

X_off = sm.add_constant(df['offsets'])
res_off = sm.OLS(df['co2_reduction'], X_off).fit()

# MLR fit
X_mlr  = sm.add_constant(df[['process', 'efficiency', 'offsets']])
res_mlr = sm.OLS(df['co2_reduction'], X_mlr).fit()

b0_mlr = res_mlr.params['const']
b1_mlr = res_mlr.params['process']
b2_mlr = res_mlr.params['efficiency']
b3_mlr = res_mlr.params['offsets']

# R² / RSE comparison
model_slr    = LinearRegression().fit(df[['process']], df['co2_reduction'])
model_mlr_sk = LinearRegression().fit(df[['process', 'efficiency', 'offsets']], df['co2_reduction'])
r2_slr  = model_slr.score(df[['process']], df['co2_reduction'])
r2_mlr  = model_mlr_sk.score(df[['process', 'efficiency', 'offsets']], df['co2_reduction'])
rse_slr = np.sqrt(np.sum((df['co2_reduction'] - model_slr.predict(df[['process']]))**2) / (n - 2))
rse_mlr = np.sqrt(np.sum((df['co2_reduction'] - model_mlr_sk.predict(df[['process', 'efficiency', 'offsets']]))**2) / (n - 4))

# All 8 models RSS
predictors_list = ['process', 'efficiency', 'offsets']
all_models_rows = []
for r in range(0, len(predictors_list) + 1):
    for subset in combinations(predictors_list, r):
        if len(subset) == 0:
            y_hat_null = np.full(n, df['co2_reduction'].mean())
            rss = np.sum((df['co2_reduction'] - y_hat_null)**2)
            all_models_rows.append({'Predictors': '(intercept only)', 'RSS': round(rss, 1)})
        else:
            X_tmp = sm.add_constant(df[list(subset)])
            res_tmp = sm.OLS(df['co2_reduction'], X_tmp).fit()
            rss = np.sum(res_tmp.resid**2)
            all_models_rows.append({'Predictors': ' + '.join(subset), 'RSS': round(rss, 1)})

all_models_df = pd.DataFrame(all_models_rows)

def fmt_p(p):
    return '< 0.0001' if p < 0.0001 else f'{p:.4f}'
```

## {#title-slide data-menu-title="Title Slide" background="#053660"}

[EDS 232]{.custom-title}

<hr class="hr-teal">

[Lesson 4]{.custom-subtitle}

[*Multiple linear regression*]{.custom-subtitle}

---

## {#in-this-lesson data-menu-title="In this lesson"}

[In this lesson]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

- The multiple linear regression (MLR) model
- Comparing MLR to individual SLRs
- Hypothesis testing and the $F$-statistic
- Model fit: $R^2$ in MLR and adjusted $R^2$
- Variable selection: forward, backward, and mixed

---

## {#section-example data-menu-title="# A guiding example #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">A guiding example</p>
</div>

---

## {#greenabate data-menu-title="The GreenAbate dataset"}

[Our example dataset]{.slide-title}

<hr>

A research consortium tracks **200 manufacturing firms**. For each firm, they record annual spending on three emissions-reduction strategies and the resulting CO₂ savings:

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
- **`process`** — investment in cleaner production processes (\$K/year)
- **`efficiency`** — investment in energy efficiency upgrades (\$K/year)
- **`offsets`** — carbon offset purchases (\$K/year)
- **`co2_reduction`** — annual CO₂ emissions reduced (ktCO₂e/year)
:::


The researchers want to understand:

::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text}
*Do these investments actually drive emissions reductions, and if so, by how much?*
:::

---

## {#greenabate-plots data-menu-title="GreenAbate: data overview"}

[CO₂ reduction vs. each predictor]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "100%"

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(fig_size_x * 1.5, fig_size_y))

specs = [
    ('process',    'steelblue',  'Process upgrades ($K)'),
    ('efficiency', 'darkorange', 'Efficiency upgrades ($K)'),
    ('offsets',    'seagreen',   'Carbon offsets ($K)'),
]

for ax, (col, color, xlabel) in zip(axes, specs):
    ax.scatter(df[col], df['co2_reduction'], s=12, alpha=0.5, color=color)
    ax.set_xlabel(xlabel)
    ax.set_ylabel('CO₂ reduction (ktCO₂e)')
    ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

::: {.center-text .body-text-s}
*Synthetic data generated for educational purposes only*
:::

---

## {#mlr-motivation data-menu-title="From SLR to MLR"}

[From SLR to MLR]{.slide-title}

<hr>

In SLR we modelled the relationship between **one** predictor and a response: 

$$\texttt{co2_reduction} \approx \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 \cdot \texttt{process}.$$



Our example dataset has three potential predictors. 

Running separate SLRs has the problem that each SLR ignores the other predictors.

<br>

::: {.center-text .teal-text .body-text-m}
**Multiple linear regression** handles all predictors simultaneously.
:::


---

## {#section-mlr data-menu-title="# The MLR model #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">The multiple linear regression model</p>
</div>

---

## {#mlr-model data-menu-title="The MLR model"}

[The MLR model]{.slide-title}

<hr>

With $p$ predictors $X_1, \ldots, X_p$ and response variable $Y$, the **multiple linear regression (MLR) model** assumes the predictors and response are related by:

$$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p + \epsilon,$$

each $\beta_i$ is a **coefficient** and $\epsilon$ is an error term. 

. . .

In our example data

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
- $Y =$ `co2_reduction` is the response variable
- $p = 3$ is the number of predictors
:::


::: {.column width="50%"}
- $X_1 =$ `process` is predictor 1
- $X_2 =$ `efficiency` is predictor 2
- $X_3 =$ `offset` is predictor 3
:::

:::

So the MLR model takes the form:

$$\texttt{co2_reduction} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \texttt{process} + \beta_2 \cdot \texttt{efficiency} + \beta_3 \cdot \texttt{offsets} + \epsilon.$$


---

## {#estimating-coefficients data-menu-title="The MLR model"}

[Estimating the MLR coefficients]{.slide-title}

<hr>

In the MLR model we assume: $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p + \epsilon.$

Our goal is to estimate the coefficients and obtain a model

$$\hat{Y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 X_1 + \hat{\beta}_2 X_2 + \cdots + \hat{\beta}_p X_p.$$

Our training set is $\{(x_1, y_1), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)\}$ with $n$ observations.

For each of the observations $(x_i, y_i)$ we have that:

- $x_i = (x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \ldots, x_{ip})$ is a vector of $p$ predictor values, and 
- $y_i$ is the response associated to $x_i$.

We estimate the coefficients/fit the model by finding the $\hat{\beta}_i$ that minimize the residual sum of squares (same procedure as SLR!):

$$
RSS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{\beta}_0 - \hat{\beta}_1x_{i1} - \ldots - \hat{\beta}_px_{ip})^2 .
$$


---

## {#mlr-coeffs data-menu-title="The MLR model"}

[Interpreting the MLR coefficients]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<div class="page-center vertical-center">

Once we find the coefficients that minimize the RSS we obtain a model:

$$\hat{Y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 X_1 + \hat{\beta}_2 X_2 + \cdots + \hat{\beta}_p X_p$$

We interpret each coefficient as

> *$\hat{\beta}_j$ is the average change in $Y$ associated with a one-unit increase in $X_j$, **holding all other predictors constant**.*

</div>

---

## {#check-in-coeffs data-menu-title="The MLR model"}

[Check-in]{.slide-title}

<hr>

For our example data, the estimated coefficients are:

```{python}
comp_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Predictor':   ['process', 'efficiency', 'offsets'],
    'MLR coeff':   [f"{res_mlr.params['process']:.4f}",
                    f"{res_mlr.params['efficiency']:.4f}",
                    f"{res_mlr.params['offsets']:.4f}"]
})
print(comp_df.to_string(index=False))
```

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}


::: {.teal-text}
1. What is the interpretation of the `efficiency` coefficient of `{python} f"{b2_mlr:.4f}"`?
2. How do we calculate the predicted CO₂ reduction for a firm that spends $100K on process upgrades, $20K on efficiency, and $50K on offsets?
3. The `offsets` coefficient is very small and negative. What does that mean?
:::

:::


::: {.column width="50%"}

:::


:::

---

## {#check-in-coeffs-answers data-menu-title="The MLR model"}

[Check-in]{.slide-title}

<hr>

For our example data, the estimated coefficients are:

```{python}
comp_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Predictor':   ['process', 'efficiency', 'offsets'],
    'MLR coeff':   [f"{res_mlr.params['process']:.4f}",
                    f"{res_mlr.params['efficiency']:.4f}",
                    f"{res_mlr.params['offsets']:.4f}"]
})
print(comp_df.to_string(index=False))
```

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.teal-text}
1. What is the interpretation of the `efficiency` coefficient of `{python} f"{b2_mlr:.4f}"`?
2. How do we calculate the predicted CO₂ reduction for a firm that spends $100K on process upgrades, $20K on efficiency, and $50K on offsets?
3. The `offsets` coefficient is very small and negative. What does that mean?
:::

:::


::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.body-text-s}
1. Each additional \$1K in energy efficiency upgrades is associated with `{python} f"{b2_mlr:.4f}"` ktCO₂e of additional CO₂ reduction, *holding process and offset spending constant*.

2. Using our model, the estimate is given by $\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 \cdot 100 + \hat{\beta}_2 \cdot 20 + \hat{\beta}_3 \cdot 50 \approx 11.29$ ktCO₂e.

3. It means if we hold process and efficiency spending constant, higher offset purchases are associated with a *slightly lower* CO₂ reduction.
:::

:::

:::
---

## {#coefficients-accuracy data-menu-title="Accuracy of the coefficient estimates"}

[Accuracy of the coefficient estimates]{.slide-title}

<hr>

Information about standard errors and confidence intervals for the MLR coefficients. 

---

## {#pvalues-mlr data-menu-title="Individual p-values in MLR"}

[$p$-values for the coefficients]{.slide-title}

<hr>

In SLR, the $p$-value for $\hat{\beta}_1$ tests $H_0: \beta_1 = 0$, which means *is $X$ related to $Y$?*

. . .

In MLR, the $p$-value for each $\hat{\beta}_j$ tests $H_0: \beta_i =0$, which means *is $X_i$ related to $Y$ if all other predictor are in the model?* 


So $H_0$ for MLR is about the **partial effect** of $X_j$. We are trying to answer:

::: {.center-text}
*Does $X_j$ add information beyond what the other predictors already explain?*
:::

<br>

. . .

We can interpret the individual $p$-values from the MLR as

- Small $p$-value for $\hat{\beta}_j$ → $X_j$ is informative **given the other predictors are in the model**
- Large $p$-value for $\hat{\beta}_j$ → $X_j$ adds little beyond what the others already capture

---

## {#check-in-p-values data-menu-title="Check-in"}

[Check-in]{.slide-title}

<hr>

For our example data we have that:

```{python}
comp_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Predictor':   ['process', 'efficiency', 'offsets'],
    'MLR coeff':   [f"{res_mlr.params['process']:.4f}",
                    f"{res_mlr.params['efficiency']:.4f}",
                    f"{res_mlr.params['offsets']:.4f}"],
    'MLR p-value': [fmt_p(res_mlr.pvalues['process']),
                    fmt_p(res_mlr.pvalues['efficiency']),
                    fmt_p(res_mlr.pvalues['offsets'])],
})
print(comp_df.to_string(index=False))
```


<br>

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.teal-text}
1. How can we interpret the $p$-value associated with the `process` variable?

2. The `offsets` variable has a large $p$-value of `{python} fmt_p(res_mlr.pvalues['offsets'])`. What does it tell us in the context of this MLR model?
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

:::

:::


---

## {#check-in-p-values-answers data-menu-title="Check-in"}

[Check-in]{.slide-title}

<hr>

For our example data we have that:

```{python}
comp_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Predictor':   ['process', 'efficiency', 'offsets'],
    'MLR coeff':   [f"{res_mlr.params['process']:.4f}",
                    f"{res_mlr.params['efficiency']:.4f}",
                    f"{res_mlr.params['offsets']:.4f}"],
    'MLR p-value': [fmt_p(res_mlr.pvalues['process']),
                    fmt_p(res_mlr.pvalues['efficiency']),
                    fmt_p(res_mlr.pvalues['offsets'])],
})
print(comp_df.to_string(index=False))
```


<br>

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.teal-text}
1. How can we interpret the $p$-value associated with the `process` variable?

2. The `offsets` variable has a large $p$-value of `{python} fmt_p(res_mlr.pvalues['offsets'])`. What does it tell us in the context of this MLR model?
:::
:::


::: {.column width="50%"}
1. There is a relationship between the `process` predictor and the response variable `co2_reduction`, when the `efficiency` and `offsets` remain fixed. 

2. It is detecting that `offset` spending contributes almost nothing beyond what `process` and `efficiency` already capture.
:::

:::

---

## {#section-comparison data-menu-title="# MLR vs. individual SLRs #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Comparing MLR to individual SLRs</p>
</div>

---

## {#slr-vs-mlr-1 data-menu-title="SLR vs. MLR coefficients"}

[SLR vs. MLR: why not just run $p$ separate regressions?]{.slide-title}

<hr>

- **individual SLR coefficients can be misleading when predictors are correlated**
- When two predictors are correlated, each one's SLR coefficient absorbs part of the other's effect. 

*Example*

Suppose firms that invest heavily in `process` upgrades tend to also invest heavily in `efficiency`.

- An SLR of `co2_reduction` on `process` alone will give a coefficient that also reflects some of the `efficiency` effect.
- The MLR coefficient for `process` strips out the `efficiency` effect and reflects only the direct relationship of `process`.

---

## {#slr-vs-mlr-2 data-menu-title="SLR vs. MLR coefficients"}

[SLR vs. MLR: why not just run $p$ separate regressions?]{.slide-title}

<hr>

- **individual SLR coefficients can be misleading when predictors are correlated**
- When two predictors are correlated, each one's SLR coefficient absorbs part of the other's effect. 

*Example*

::: {.body-text-s}
In our example dataset, SLR ≈ MLR coefficients because the predictors were drawn independently (uncorrelated by construction).
:::

```{python}
#| echo: false

comp_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Predictor':   ['process', 'efficiency', 'offsets'],
    'SLR coeff':   [f"{res_proc.params['process']:.4f}",
                    f"{res_eff.params['efficiency']:.4f}",
                    f"{res_off.params['offsets']:.4f}"],
    'SLR p-value': [fmt_p(res_proc.pvalues['process']),
                    fmt_p(res_eff.pvalues['efficiency']),
                    fmt_p(res_off.pvalues['offsets'])],
    'MLR coeff':   [f"{res_mlr.params['process']:.4f}",
                    f"{res_mlr.params['efficiency']:.4f}",
                    f"{res_mlr.params['offsets']:.4f}"],
    'MLR p-value': [fmt_p(res_mlr.pvalues['process']),
                    fmt_p(res_mlr.pvalues['efficiency']),
                    fmt_p(res_mlr.pvalues['offsets'])],
})
print(comp_df.to_string(index=False))
```

::: {.body-text-s}
- In real data, predictors can be correlated. The divergence between SLR and MLR coefficients can be large.
- The MLR coefficient reflects the *direct* effect of each predictor, holding the others constant.
:::

---

## {#section-fstat data-menu-title="# Hypothesis testing #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Hypothesis testing & the F-statistic</p>
</div>

---

## {#hypotheses-mlr data-menu-title="All coefficients test in MLR"}

[Is any predictor related to the response?]{.slide-title}

<hr>

In SLR: $Y \approx \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$ and we tested

- $H_0$: $\beta_1=0 \Rightarrow$ no relationship between response and predictor
- $H_a$: $\beta_1\neq0 \Rightarrow$ some relationship between response and predictor. 

<br>

. . .

For MLR: $Y \approx \beta_0 + \beta_1X_1 + \ldots + \beta_p X_p$ and we can test

- $H_0$: $\beta_1 =... =\beta_p=0 \Rightarrow$ no relationship between $Y$ and *any* predictor $X_1, \ldots, X_p$
- $H_a$: at least one $\beta_j \neq 0 \Rightarrow$ some relationship between response and *some* predictor. 

<br>

We are investigating the question *Is any predictor related to the response?*


---

## {#f-statistic data-menu-title="The F-statistic"}

[The F-statistic]{.slide-title}

<hr>

We are investigating the question *Is any predictor related to the response?*


We test this with the **$F$-statistic**:

$$F = \frac{(TSS - RSS)/p}{RSS/(n - p - 1)}$$

::: {.body-text-s}
- $TSS = \sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ is the total variability in $Y$,
- $RSS = \sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ is the variability unexplained by the model,
:::

. . .

::: {.body-text-s}
- *Numerator*: how much variance the predictors collectively explain 
- *Denominator*: how much variance remains unexplained per degree of freedom
:::

. . .

::: {.body-text-s}
- $F \approx 1$: no predictor is related to $Y$ → consistent with $H_0$
- $F \gg 1$: at least one predictor explains variance beyond noise → evidence against $H_0$
:::

. . .

An associated $p$-value is then computed to assess the probability of observing an $F$-statistic this large or larger, assuming $H_0$ is true. 

---

## {#fstat-greenabate data-menu-title="F-statistic example"}

[Example]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{python}
#| echo: false

fstat_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Quantity': ['F-statistic', 'p-value'],
    'Value':    [f'{res_mlr.fvalue:.2f}', fmt_p(res_mlr.f_pvalue)]
})
print(fstat_df.to_string(index=False))
```


<br>

An $F$-statistic of `{python} f"{res_mlr.fvalue:.1f}"` with $p \ll 0.05$ gives very strong evidence that at least one predictor is associated with CO₂ reductions.

<br>

. . .

::: {.teal-text}
**Check-in**

Pair up with someone. Have one person explain the hypothesis test for simple linear regression and the other explain the hypothesis test for multiple linear regression.
:::

---

## {#hypothesis-testing data-menu-title="Hypothesis testing framework"}

[Hypothesis testing steps]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-s}
From EDS 222.
:::

::: {.body-text-m}

1. Identify the TEST STATISTIC

2. State your NULL and ALTERNATIVE hypotheses

3. Calculate the OBSERVED test statistic

4. Estimate the NULL DISTRIBUTION

5. Calculate P-VALUE

6. Compare p-value to CRITICAL THRESHOLD

:::

---

## {#individual-p-values-vs-f-stat data-menu-title="Individual p-values"}

[Why not just use individual p-values?]{.slide-title}

<hr>

Earlier we saw that each coefficient of the MLR has an associated $p$-value. So one my ask: why do we need a $p$-value for the $F$-statistic, when we can just interpet each $p$-value individually?

. . .

For an individual coefficient, a $p$-value of 0.05 means: 

> "if there is truly no relationship ($H_0$ true), there is a 5% probability of getting a $t$-statistic this extreme just by random chance."

Suppose $p=100$ and there's truly no association between any predictor and the response. We would expect about 5% of our $t$-statistics to be "false positives" just by random chance. 

. . .

When $p$ is large, we run the risk of some individual $p$-values being small *by chance* even when no predictor is truly related to $Y$. 

The $F$-statistic tests all coefficients jointly and accounts for this.

---

## {#section-modelfit data-menu-title="# Model fit #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Model fit: R² in MLR</p>
</div>

---

## {#r2-mlr data-menu-title="R² in MLR"}

[$R^2$ in multiple regression]{.slide-title}

<hr>

The $R^2$ formula is the same as in SLR:

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{RSS}{TSS}$$

It measures the proportion of variance in $Y$ explained by the model. 

<br>

*Caveat*: adding a predictor to an MLR model will increase $R^2$ or leave it unchanged, even if that predictor has no real relationship with $Y$.

. . .

<br>

The **adjusted $R^2$** penalizes for the number of predictors:

$$\bar{R}^2 = 1 - \frac{RSS/(n-p-1)}{TSS/(n-1)}$$

Adjusted $R^2$ can decrease when an irrelevant predictor is added. Better for comparing models of different sizes.

---

## {#r2-comparison data-menu-title="R² comparison: SLR vs MLR"}

[Comparing SLR and MLR model fit]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{python}
#| echo: false

acc_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Model':  ['SLR (process only)', 'MLR (all three predictors)'],
    'R²':     [f'{r2_slr:.3f}', f'{r2_mlr:.3f}'],
    'RSE (ktCO₂e)': [f'{rse_slr:.3f}', f'{rse_mlr:.3f}'],
})
print(acc_df.to_string(index=False))
```

. . .

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
- $R^2$ increased from `{python} f"{r2_slr:.3f}"` to `{python} f"{r2_mlr:.3f}"` — `efficiency` is genuinely predictive.
- RSE decreased from `{python} f"{rse_slr:.3f}"` to `{python} f"{rse_mlr:.3f}"` ktCO₂e — predictions are more accurate with all three predictors.
:::

---

## {#section-varsel data-menu-title="# Variable selection #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Variable selection</p>
</div>

---

## {#varsel-problem data-menu-title="The variable selection problem"}

[Which predictors should we include?]{.slide-title}

<hr>

With $p$ predictors there are $2^p$ possible ways of choosing which predictors to use:

- $p = 3$: 8 models — feasible to compare exhaustively
- $p = 10$: 1,024 models
- $p = 30$: over 1 billion models — completely infeasible

<br>

. . .

Three common automated approaches for large $p$:

- [**Forward selection**]{.teal-text}: start with nothing, add one predictor at a time
- [**Backward selection**]{.teal-text}: start with everything, remove one at a time
- [**Mixed selection**]{.teal-text}: combine forward and backward steps

---

## {#forward-backward-1 data-menu-title="Forward and backward selection"}

[Forward and backward selection]{.slide-title}

<hr>

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Forward selection**

1. Start: null model (intercept only)
2. Add the predictor with the largest RSS reduction
3. Repeat until some stopping criterion is met

✓ Always applicable 

✗ A variable added early may become redundant later
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

:::

::::

---

## {#forward-backward-2 data-menu-title="Forward and backward selection"}

[Forward and backward selection]{.slide-title}

<hr>

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Forward selection**

1. Start: null model (intercept only)
2. Add the predictor with the largest RSS reduction
3. Repeat until some stopping criterion is met

✓ Always applicable 

✗ A variable added early may become redundant later
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Backward selection**

1. Start: full model (all $p$ predictors)
2. Remove the predictor with the largest $p$-value
3. Repeat until all remaining predictors are significant

✓ Considers all predictors from the start

✗ Cannot be used when $p > n$
:::

::::

---

## {#mixed-selection data-menu-title="Mixed selection"}

[Mixed selection]{.slide-title}

<hr>

**Mixed selection** (stepwise) combines forward and backward steps:

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
1. Start with the null model
2. **Forward step**: add the variable with the largest RSS reduction
3. **Backward step**: check if any included variable now has a large $p$-value — if so, remove it
4. Continue until no variables are added or removed
:::

. . .

<br>

::: {.body-text-m .teal-text}
This fixes the main weakness of forward selection: a variable added early can be *removed* once other, better variables are in the model.
:::


---

## {#all-8-models data-menu-title="Check-on"}

[Check-in]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{python}
#| echo: false
print(all_models_df.to_string(index=False))
```

. . .

<br>

::: {.teal-text .body-text-m}
Trace through forward selection using this table.

- Which predictor would be added first? Second? Does adding `offsets` meaningfully reduce RSS? 

- What criteria could be used to decide whether to add or not the final predictor?
:::

---

## {#checkin-forward-sel data-menu-title="Check-in: forward selection" background="#053660"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Check-in — forward selection answers</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">Step 1: `process` has the lowest single-predictor RSS → added first.</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">Step 2: `process + efficiency` has lower RSS than `process + offsets` → `efficiency` added second.</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">Step 3: adding `offsets` gives only a tiny further reduction — whether it is included depends on the stopping criterion.</p>
</div>


---

## {#other-metrics data-menu-title="Other selection criteria"}

[Other criteria for variable selection]{.slide-title}

<hr>

RSS alone always favors larger models — use criteria that **penalize complexity**:

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
| Criterion | Formula | Goal |
|-----------|---------|------|
| Adjusted $R^2$ | $1 - \frac{RSS/(n-p-1)}{TSS/(n-1)}$ | Maximize |
| AIC | $n\log(RSS/n) + 2p$ | Minimize |
| BIC | $n\log(RSS/n) + p\log(n)$ | Minimize |
| Test MSE (CV) | held-out set error | Minimize |
:::

. . .

<br>

::: {.body-text-s}
BIC penalizes extra predictors more heavily than AIC (for $n > 7$), tending to select sparser models. Cross-validation is the most direct approach when the goal is prediction accuracy.
:::

---

## {#recap data-menu-title="Recap"}

[In this lesson we covered]{.slide-title}

<hr>

- **MLR model**: $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p + \epsilon$ — coefficients interpreted *holding all else constant*
- Least squares minimizes RSS; computer solves it
- **Individual p-values**: test partial effects (does $X_j$ add beyond the others?)
- **F-statistic**: tests $H_0: \beta_1 = \cdots = \beta_p = 0$ — use before relying on individual p-values
- **$R^2$** always increases with predictors; use **adjusted $R^2$** to compare models of different sizes
- **Variable selection**: forward, backward, mixed — use AIC, BIC, adjusted $R^2$, or CV as criteria
